APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work squarely targets core NLP research on sequence-to-sequence generation and information extraction, proposing an encoder-decoder+copy model and a large training corpus that are directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML conferences. The paper uses community-expected methods and evaluation (bi-GRU encoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search, multiple standard datasets including a large new KP20k test set, and macro P/R/F metrics), compares to appropriate baselines, and reports hyperparameters—making it suitable for the venue’s audience. From an experimentalist perspective the scale of data and explicit evaluation setup further strengthen its fit for a top conference. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a novel encoder–decoder variant (CopyRNN with attention and copy) for keyphrase generation and benchmarks it against standard unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui) showing substantial gains. The large-scale metadata corpus (567k articles with a 20k-article KP20k test set) is a significant resource contribution that meaningfully improves evaluation coverage for present and absent keyphrases. Methodology (seq2seq + copy, beam search) and evaluation (macro P/R/F on multiple established datasets) meet community standards, and the work is well positioned to impact retrieval/indexing applications for scientific text.

From an experimentalist perspective the scale of data and explicit evaluation setup further strengthen its fit for a top conference.

The large-scale metadata corpus (567k articles with a 20k-article KP20k test set) is a significant resource contribution that meaningfully improves evaluation coverage for present and absent keyphrases. Methodology (seq2seq + copy, beam search) and evaluation (macro P/R/F on multiple established datasets) meet community standards, and the work is well positioned to impact retrieval/indexing applications for scientific text.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper makes a notable, but not radical, contribution: it applies an encoder–decoder with attention plus a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to keyphrase generation and introduces a large KP20k test corpus (20k papers) and a huge training pool—both clearly distinguish it from earlier seq2seq work (e.g., convolutional seq2seq and prior Deep Keyphrase Generation). However, sequence-to-sequence generation for keyphrases was already explored in closely related contemporaneous work, so the methodological novelty is a non-trivial engineering/architectural extension (copy mechanism + large-scale data) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. An expert would be informed by the dataset and the copy-based adaptation, but would not find the overall idea surprising given the cited prior art. | B(3): From the provided summary, the paper makes a moderately novel contribution: it applies an encoder–decoder framework with an added copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to generate both present and absent keyphrases and assembles a large KP20k test corpus (20k articles). However, closely related neural seq2seq approaches to keyphrase generation (including RNN/CNN sequence-to-sequence models cited in the literature search) already address the same task, so the core formulation is well-studied. The copy mechanism and the large-scale KP20k dataset are useful, non-trivial extensions that improve practical performance and evaluation coverage, but they do not constitute a highly original paradigm shift beyond existing deep keyphrase generation work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes a notable, but not radical, contribution: it applies an encoder–decoder with attention plus a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to keyphrase generation and introduces a large KP20k test corpus (20k papers) and a huge training pool—both clearly distinguish it from earlier seq2seq work (e.g., convolutional seq2seq and prior Deep Keyphrase Generation). However, sequence-to-sequence generation for keyphrases was already explored in closely related contemporaneous work, so the methodological novelty is a non-trivial engineering/architectural extension (copy mechanism + large-scale data) rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. An expert would be informed by the dataset and the copy-based adaptation, but would not find the overall idea surprising given the cited prior art.

reviewer_b.justification: From the provided summary, the paper makes a moderately novel contribution: it applies an encoder–decoder framework with an added copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to generate both present and absent keyphrases and assembles a large KP20k test corpus (20k articles). However, closely related neural seq2seq approaches to keyphrase generation (including RNN/CNN sequence-to-sequence models cited in the literature search) already address the same task, so the core formulation is well-studied. The copy mechanism and the large-scale KP20k dataset are useful, non-trivial extensions that improve practical performance and evaluation coverage, but they do not constitute a highly original paradigm shift beyond existing deep keyphrase generation work.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important and practical problem—generating both present and absent keyphrases—using a novel application of encoder–decoder models with a copy mechanism and demonstrates large empirical gains (over 20% on present-keyphrase extraction) across multiple benchmarks, which is likely to change how researchers approach keyphrase generation versus traditional extraction. The release of a large training corpus and a 20k-article test set (KP20k) further boosts impact by enabling more comprehensive evaluation and follow-up work. While not entirely transformative given similarities to existing seq2seq paradigms, the clear empirical improvements and new dataset make this a strong contribution that will be built upon by others in the area. | B(4): The paper addresses a practically important problem—predicting both present and absent keyphrases for scientific documents—and supplies a scalable, neural solution (CopyRNN) that outperforms strong extraction baselines by over 20% on present-keyphrase prediction. The release of a large-scale training corpus (≈527k articles) and a 20k-article test set (KP20k) materially improves evaluation coverage and will enable reproducible comparison and transfer learning. While the model architecture adapts known encoder–decoder and copy mechanisms rather than introducing radically new modeling paradigms, the empirical gains and dataset resource are likely to change practitioner practices and spur follow-up work on generative keyphrase models and metadata-driven applications.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important and practical problem—generating both present and absent keyphrases—using a novel application of encoder–decoder models with a copy mechanism and demonstrates large empirical gains (over 20% on present-keyphrase extraction) across multiple benchmarks, which is likely to change how researchers approach keyphrase generation versus traditional extraction. The release of a large training corpus and a 20k-article test set (KP20k) further boosts impact by enabling more comprehensive evaluation and follow-up work. While not entirely transformative given similarities to existing seq2seq paradigms, the clear empirical improvements and new dataset make this a strong contribution that will be built upon by others in the area.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a practically important problem—predicting both present and absent keyphrases for scientific documents—and supplies a scalable, neural solution (CopyRNN) that outperforms strong extraction baselines by over 20% on present-keyphrase prediction. The release of a large-scale training corpus (≈527k articles) and a 20k-article test set (KP20k) materially improves evaluation coverage and will enable reproducible comparison and transfer learning. While the model architecture adapts known encoder–decoder and copy mechanisms rather than introducing radically new modeling paradigms, the empirical gains and dataset resource are likely to change practitioner practices and spur follow-up work on generative keyphrase models and metadata-driven applications.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with large-scale experiments across multiple datasets (including the new KP20k) showing substantial gains (≈20%) over a range of baselines, and presents a sensible CopyRNN architecture with attention and copying. However, from the provided summary important experimental details appear missing for full technical validation: I see no mention of multiple random runs or statistical significance tests, limited ablation analyses isolating the copy mechanism/beam-size effects, and no confirmation that code or preprocessing steps are released for reproducibility. Mathematical content is standard Seq2Seq and appears correct, so the issues are principally around experimental robustness and reproducibility rather than fundamental correctness, hence a score of 4. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: an encoder-decoder with attention+copy (CopyRNN) is evaluated against a broad set of unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) and shows substantial gains (reported >20% on average for present-keyphrase extraction) on multiple standard datasets plus a new large KP20k test set. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify and the model description (Bi-GRU encoder, GRU decoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search) is internally consistent; hyperparameters are reported. Methodologically it is largely sound, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or ablations beyond noting OOV and generic-generation failure modes, which are minor gaps in experimental rigor. Overall the results justify the conclusions though a couple of additional analyses (significance, deeper ablation on copy vs. attention) would strengthen the technical case.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with large-scale experiments across multiple datasets (including the new KP20k) showing substantial gains (≈20%) over a range of baselines, and presents a sensible CopyRNN architecture with attention and copying. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify and the model description (Bi-GRU encoder, GRU decoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search) is internally consistent; hyperparameters are reported. Methodologically it is largely sound, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or ablations beyond noting OOV and generic-generation failure modes, which are minor gaps in experimental rigor. Overall the results justify the conclusions though a couple of additional analyses (significance, deeper ablation on copy vs. attention) would strengthen the technical case.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear progression (intro → model → experiments → conclusion) with the main contributions stated early, as indicated by the explicit presentation of the CopyRNN variant and the KP20k test set. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective and informative which supports reproducibility-focused readers like me. Writing quality is good with little ambiguity, though the summary does not mention code release (useful for full reproducibility) — this is a minor presentation/availability point rather than a clarity flaw. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the CopyRNN model and the new KP20k test set. Notation and methodology (encoder–decoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search) are presented clearly, hyperparameters and datasets are reported, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples appear informative and the writing quality is good overall, though a few minor presentation details (e.g., explicit mention of code availability and more discussion of illustrative examples) could improve reproducibility and reader guidance.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear progression (intro → model → experiments → conclusion) with the main contributions stated early, as indicated by the explicit presentation of the CopyRNN variant and the KP20k test set. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective and informative which supports reproducibility-focused readers like me. Writing quality is good with little ambiguity, though the summary does not mention code release (useful for full reproducibility) — this is a minor presentation/availability point rather than a clarity flaw.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the CopyRNN model and the new KP20k test set. Notation and methodology (encoder–decoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search) are presented clearly, hyperparameters and datasets are reported, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples appear informative and the writing quality is good overall, though a few minor presentation details (e.g., explicit mention of code availability and more discussion of illustrative examples) could improve reproducibility and reader guidance.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of classical unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) on multiple standard datasets and the new large KP20k split, using consistent metrics (precision/recall/F1), and reports hyperparameters—supporting generally fair experimental conditions. However, given closely related recent neural work on deep keyphrase generation (e.g., convolutional seq2seq and other 2017 deep keyphrase-generation models), the paper does not appear to compare directly against these contemporary neural baselines; this omission weakens claims of superiority for the proposed CopyRNN. The paper does acknowledge some limitations (e.g., OOV issues, failure modes of plain RNN) and discusses ExpandRank’s scalability problems, but a direct head-to-head with the most relevant recent seq2seq approaches and clearer ablations isolating the copy mechanism would be needed for a comprehensive comparison. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of classical unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) on standard datasets and the new KP20k using the same metrics (precision/recall/F1), and reports hyperparameters, so comparisons are largely fair. However, it appears to omit direct empirical comparisons to closely related recent neural sequence-to-sequence approaches (e.g., convolutional seq2seq and other contemporary deep keyphrase generation models from 2017), which would be the most relevant competing baselines for a copy-equipped encoder–decoder. The authors do acknowledge limitations of the plain RNN generative model and discuss prior work thoroughly, but the lack of head-to-head results against those neural baselines is a notable omission for full completeness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of classical unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) on multiple standard datasets and the new large KP20k split, using consistent metrics (precision/recall/F1), and reports hyperparameters—supporting generally fair experimental conditions. However, given closely related recent neural work on deep keyphrase generation (e.g., convolutional seq2seq and other 2017 deep keyphrase-generation models), the paper does not appear to compare directly against these contemporary neural baselines; this omission weakens claims of superiority for the proposed CopyRNN. The paper does acknowledge some limitations (e.g., OOV issues, failure modes of plain RNN) and discusses ExpandRank’s scalability problems, but a direct head-to-head with the most relevant recent seq2seq approaches and clearer ablations isolating the copy mechanism would be needed for a comprehensive comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of classical unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) on standard datasets and the new KP20k using the same metrics (precision/recall/F1), and reports hyperparameters, so comparisons are largely fair. However, it appears to omit direct empirical comparisons to closely related recent neural sequence-to-sequence approaches (e.g., convolutional seq2seq and other contemporary deep keyphrase generation models from 2017), which would be the most relevant competing baselines for a copy-equipped encoder–decoder. The authors do acknowledge limitations of the plain RNN generative model and discuss prior work thoroughly, but the lack of head-to-head results against those neural baselines is a notable omission for full completeness.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides substantial empirical evaluation: a very large training corpus, a new 20k-article test set (KP20k), and comparisons to a broad set of unsupervised and supervised baselines showing large gains for the CopyRNN. Related work coverage appears thorough and the authors report hyperparameters. However, important experimental controls are missing: no clear ablation/sensitivity studies isolating the copy mechanism, attention, beam size or vocabulary effects, no reported run-to-run variance or statistical significance, and code/release is not mentioned which hinders reproducibility. Failure modes and some limitations are acknowledged (OOV handling, generic generations), but the experimental analysis lacks the depth (ablations, significance, robustness) I would require to fully support the claimed contributions and outperforming prior neural approaches (e.g., other seq2seq variants). | B(4): The paper provides a substantial empirical evaluation: it trains on a very large scientific corpus (≈527k articles), introduces a large KP20k test set (20k), and compares CopyRNN against a range of unsupervised and supervised baselines, reporting sizable gains (>20% for present keyphrase extraction). It reports hyperparameters and notes concrete failure modes (overly general outputs, OOV issues, ExpandRank scaling problems), and positions itself against prior deep keyphrase work. However, important analyses are missing or limited: there is no explicit ablation/sensitivity study isolating the contribution of the copy mechanism, vocabulary/beam-size/training-data scaling, nor direct comparison to other neural seq2seq variants (e.g., convolutional seq2seq) mentioned in related literature. Reproducibility is partly supported by dataset and hyperparameter reporting but weakened by no mention of released code. These strengths and gaps justify a solid but not perfect score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides substantial empirical evaluation: a very large training corpus, a new 20k-article test set (KP20k), and comparisons to a broad set of unsupervised and supervised baselines showing large gains for the CopyRNN. Related work coverage appears thorough and the authors report hyperparameters. However, important experimental controls are missing: no clear ablation/sensitivity studies isolating the copy mechanism, attention, beam size or vocabulary effects, no reported run-to-run variance or statistical significance, and code/release is not mentioned which hinders reproducibility. Failure modes and some limitations are acknowledged (OOV handling, generic generations), but the experimental analysis lacks the depth (ablations, significance, robustness) I would require to fully support the claimed contributions and outperforming prior neural approaches (e.g., other seq2seq variants).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a substantial empirical evaluation: it trains on a very large scientific corpus (≈527k articles), introduces a large KP20k test set (20k), and compares CopyRNN against a range of unsupervised and supervised baselines, reporting sizable gains (>20% for present keyphrase extraction). It reports hyperparameters and notes concrete failure modes (overly general outputs, OOV issues, ExpandRank scaling problems), and positions itself against prior deep keyphrase work. However, important analyses are missing or limited: there is no explicit ablation/sensitivity study isolating the contribution of the copy mechanism, vocabulary/beam-size/training-data scaling, nor direct comparison to other neural seq2seq variants (e.g., convolutional seq2seq) mentioned in related literature. Reproducibility is partly supported by dataset and hyperparameter reporting but weakened by no mention of released code. These strengths and gaps justify a solid but not perfect score.

main_claims: Proposes a deep keyphrase generation model (encoder-decoder) that can generate both present (extraction) and absent (generation) keyphrases from text.

Introduces a CopyRNN variant (encoder-decoder with attention + copy mechanism) that significantly outperforms a range of unsupervised and supervised baselines on keyphrase prediction, exceeding best baselines by over 20% on average for present-keyphrase extraction.

Collects a large-scale training corpus of scientific metadata (567,830 articles, 527,830 used for model training) and introduces a new test set KP20k containing 20,000 articles to enable more comprehensive evaluation.
_summary.methodology: An RNN encoder-decoder (sequence-to-sequence) framework is used with a bidirectional GRU encoder, a forward GRU decoder, attention mechanism, and an added copy mechanism (CopyRNN); beam search is used for generation and models are jointly trained to maximize conditional probability.
datasets: Training corpus of 567,830 articles (527,830 used for training after holding out 40,000)
limitations: Plain RNN generative model tends to produce overly general keyphrases and fails to recall keyphrases containing out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words.
ExpandRank fails to return results on the large KP20k dataset due to high time complexity.
The paper notes nearly 2% of correct predictions contain OOV words (indicating vocabulary coverage issues).

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP systems, specialized in sequence models, experimental methodology, and reproducible research infrastructure.
traits: Insists on clear, reproducible experimental setup: dataset splits, preprocessing, training/validation/test separation, and hyperparameter details.
Values thorough baselines and ablation studies to attribute gains to specific model components (e.g., copy mechanism, attention, beam size).
Checks statistical significance and robustness: multiple runs, variance reporting, and sensitivity analyses to hyperparameters and dataset size.
Looks for implementation-level details that affect performance (tokenization, OOV handling, batching, early stopping) and for public release of code and data to enable replication.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the soundness and reproducibility of experiments, demanding precise comparisons and ablations that support the claimed contributions.

reviewer_b.name: Keyphrase & Applications Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with a track record in keyphrase extraction/generation, information retrieval, and applications of metadata for scientific documents; familiar with literature and practical needs of downstream users.
traits: Evaluates novelty and positioning relative to prior work on keyphrase extraction/generation and copy/seq2seq models, noting incremental vs. substantive advances.
Assesses dataset curation and representativeness: domain coverage, potential biases in scientific metadata, and whether KP20k addresses prior evaluation gaps.
Values practical impact: usefulness of generated keyphrases for search, indexing, and summarization; checks whether evaluation metrics reflect real-world utility.
Considers broader implications: scalability, annotation/label quality, and ethical/privacy concerns when harvesting large-scale metadata.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper on novelty, relevance to the research community and downstream impact, and whether the proposed contributions fill a meaningful gap in the literature.
